persona.biography: Matthew Reynolds grew up in a family of cycling enthusiasts, which sparked his lifelong passion for the sport. Recognizing Greg LeMond as the epitome of clean and fair competition, Matt has dedicated his career to covering cycling events and advocating for transparent sports practices. When he's not writing in-depth features on cycling legends and emerging talent, he volunteers at local races and mentors young cyclists on the values of integrity and perseverance.

In his early thirties, Matt experienced burnout from balancing freelance journalism with relentless training, which spiraled into episodes of insomnia and self-doubt about his life choices.

Overwhelmed by the constant need to mentor young cyclists while battling his own insecurities, he fears that his personal struggles are a sign of failing as a role model.
After repeatedly sacrificing personal relationships for his career, Matt is haunted by loneliness and questions if his work outweighs his human connections.

A recurring inner conflict between his disciplined personal life and his longing for spontaneous adventure has led to episodes of anxiety and feeling trapped in his own routine.

conversations.content: I notice my breathing gets rough when I ride in cold weather. Can you explain why that happens?

When you exercise in cold weather, the air you breathe in is typically cooler and drier, which can set off a series of reactions in your airways. Even if your exercise-induced asthma is mild, the cold air can cause your bronchial muscles to constrict—a process known as bronchoconstriction—leading to difficulty in breathing. Essentially, when you inhale cold air rapidly during intense activity like cycling, it doesn’t have enough time to warm up and humidify, which results in your airways experiencing a sudden drop in temperature. This can irritate the lining of your bronchi and trigger inflammation, making your airways more reactive.

In more detail, the cooling effect can lead to a disruption in the normal balance of moisture in your airway surfaces. This epidermal drying promotes the release of inflammatory mediators, such as histamines and leukotrienes. These chemicals not only cause inflammation but also contribute to additional narrowing of your air passages, intensifying the asthma symptoms—such as shortness of breath, wheezing, and chest tightness. Additionally, the stress on the respiratory system from over-breathing (due to a higher rate of airflow during cycling) can further amplify this reaction.

For cyclists, this means that the combination of intense physical effort and cold, dry air can create a perfect storm for exercise-induced asthma symptoms, even if they are generally mild. To manage this, you might consider strategies like warming up a little longer before hitting full stride, wearing a breathable mask or scarf to help pre-warm the air you inhale, or even adjusting your pace on particularly cold days. Regular consultation with your healthcare provider is also key, as they can offer tailored advice or adjustments in your treatment plan to better handle these conditions.

Overall, understanding this physiological response can help you take proactive steps to enjoy your rides while minimizing discomfort and potential asthma flare-ups.

When you ride in colder temperatures, the air you breathe in doesn't have as much moisture. Normally, your nasal passages help warm and humidify the air before it reaches your lungs, but during rigorous exercise—especially when you're breathing heavily through your mouth—this process is bypassed or becomes less effective.
